title: Optic Neuritis
oneLiner: Acute demyelinating inflammation of the optic nerve, typically in young adults (20–50y), strongly associated with multiple sclerosis; threatens vision but rarely fatal.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA) in patients >50y** — can rapidly cause bilateral irreversible blindness *and* aortic dissection/stroke.  
  *Single finding:* “jaw claudication”, new headache, scalp tenderness, or ESR >50 (but never delay steroids while waiting).  
- **Compressive optic neuropathy** (pituitary apoplexy, intracranial mass, aneurysm) — can cause rapid vision loss and fatal brainstem compression.  
  *Single finding:* “bitemporal hemianopia” or “other cranial nerve palsies” on examination → immediate imaging.
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** (infectious or granulomatous) — if fever, meningism, or altered consciousness, treat empirically before scanning.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute or subacute monocular vision loss (hours to days)
  - Pain on eye movement (retrobulbar pain, worse with adduction)
  - Reduced colour vision (desaturation of red) — often earliest symptom
  - “Blurring” or “fogging” in one eye; occasionally visual flashes (photopsia) with movement
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) — present in unilateral cases, even without visible optic disc swelling
  - Reduced visual acuity (Snellen, pinhole)
  - Reduced colour vision (Ishihara plates)
  - Visual field defect (central scotoma most common)
  - Fundoscopy: normal in 2/3 of cases (retrobulbar neuritis); optic disc swelling in anterior (papillitis) cases
  - No proptosis, no conjunctival injection, no ophthalmoplegia (unless MS brainstem lesion)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Optic neuritis (demyelinating):** young adult, pain on movement, RAPD, normal fundus or disc swelling, no fever — classical.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** age >50, headache, jaw claudication, ESR/CRP elevated, fundus may show pale disc/arteritic AION.
- **Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION):** older (>50), often hypertensive/diabetic, no pain, altitudinal field defect, no RAPD? (often subtle), disc oedema with haemorrhages.
- **Compressive lesion (e.g. pituitary, aneurysm, meningioma):** slow onset or apoplexy; other neuro signs (CN III, IV, VI); visual field bitemporal or homonymous; imaging essential.
- **Infectious/inflammatory (sarcoid, syphilis, lupus):** bilateral or recurrent, uveitis, systemic symptoms; serology + LP.
- **Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON):** male predominance, acute painless vision loss, no recovery, family history (maternal), mitochondrial mutation.

**Splitter:** *The single feature that bifurcates GCA from demyelinating optic neuritis:* **Age >50 + headache/jaw claudication** → treat as GCA (temporal artery biopsy, start steroids immediately). If under 40 and no systemic symptoms, it’s almost always demyelinating.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart) each eye separately
  - Colour vision (Ishihara plates) — often first to drop
  - RAPD (swinging light test) — key
  - Fundoscopy (direct ophthalmoscopy) — look for disc swelling, haemorrhages, pallor
  - Check pupils, eye movements, confrontational visual fields
  - If age >50 or suspect GCA: ESR + CRP (stat)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - Autoantibodies (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA) if suspect lupus/vasculitis
  - ACE (if sarcoid possible)
  - Syphilis serology (TPPA/VDRL)
  - Anti-MOG antibodies (if recurrent, bilateral, or treatment-resistant) — send to specialist lab
  - Consider Lyme serology (if tick exposure)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain + orbits with gadolinium (thin cuts through optic nerves) — look for optic nerve enhancement (demyelination) and white matter lesions suggestive of MS
  - **Second-line:** CT head/orbits if MRI contraindicated or to rule out compressive lesion urgently
  - **Consider:** CT venogram if suspect cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (rare)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture: if atypical features (fever, bilateral, rapid progression, immunosuppressed) — check opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands (matching CSF-serum bands suggest MS)
  - Visual evoked potentials (VEP): delayed P100 latency — often delayed even after clinical recovery; useful if diagnosis uncertain
  - Optical coherence tomography (OCT): shows retinal nerve fibre layer thinning later; not acute
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in all women of childbearing age (rules out CN II palsy from eclampsia, and affects treatment choice) **AND** **ESR/CRP in all patients >50** to not miss GCA.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If GCA suspected (age >50, headache, jaw claudication, ESR/CRP elevated) — **start steroids immediately** (prednisolone 40–60mg PO or IV methylprednisolone 500mg–1g) **before** biopsy — do not wait for results.
  - If compressive lesion suspected (other cranial nerve signs, visual field bitemporal) — urgent MRI/CT and neurosurgical referral.
  - If infection suspected (fever, meningism) — start empirical antibiotics (ceftriaxone + aciclovir) and perform LP.
  - Otherwise, for isolated demyelinating optic neuritis: no emergency intervention needed; confirm diagnosis and start steroids within 1 week if vision severely affected.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Only if vision significantly impaired** (e.g. acuity <6/12, dense field defect) or bilateral → **consider high-dose IV methylprednisolone** (1g IV daily for 3 days) followed by oral prednisolone taper (starting 50mg/day, reduce 10mg every 3 days over 2–4 weeks) — *use [check local policy] for specific steroid regimen.*
  - If no severe vision loss, no benefit from steroids — observe.
  - Monitor visual acuity daily, pain improvement.
  - Arrange MRI brain/orbits if not already done.
  - Referral to neurology / neuro-ophthalmology if available.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Once acute episode resolves, **refer to neurology** for MS risk assessment (MRI brain for dissemination in space/time, LP for oligoclonal bands)
  - If MRI shows MS‑typical lesions → consider disease‑modifying therapy (DMT) after neurology review; vitamin D supplementation often advised (evidence incomplete but safe)
  - If recurrent optic neuritis without MS → anti‑MOG antibody testing; may need immunosuppression.
  - Visual rehabilitation if residual deficit.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for steroids):**
  - Normal saline if IV methylprednisolone given; ensure good oral intake (steroids cause fluid retention and hyperglycaemia)
  - Capillary blood glucose monitoring (4‑hourly while on high-dose steroids) — check for steroid-induced diabetes
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (all admitted patients with reduced mobility)
  - Diet: no specific restriction; avoid high sugar
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, consider 4‑hourly during steroid infusion
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (severe optic neuritis):**
  - IV methylprednisolone 1g in 250mL 0.9% sodium chloride over 1–2 hours daily for 3 days. Followed by:
  - Oral prednisolone 50mg once daily for 7–10 days, then taper (e.g. reduce by 10mg every 3–4 days).
  - *Check local policy for exact oral taper regimen; some units use no taper after short IV course.*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Oral prednisolone monotherapy (1mg/kg/day for 7–14 days) if IV not appropriate — less studied but used.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Systemic steroids contraindicated in active infection (treat infection first), uncontrolled diabetes (monitor glucose closely), severe psychiatric illness (may worsen), uncontrolled hypertension.
  - Caution with NSAIDs (increased GI bleed risk with steroids).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment for methylprednisolone or prednisolone in renal impairment. Hepatic impairment may require dose reduction — discuss with pharmacy.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **“Giving oral prednisolone alone (without IV methylprednisolone) in severe acute optic neuritis with vision <6/60”** — oral steroids in high doses have been shown to increase relapse risk in MS (the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial); always use IV methylprednisolone for severe cases. Also, **failing to monitor blood glucose** in a non-diabetic patient on high-dose steroids can lead to hyperglycaemic emergencies.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Steroid-induced hyperglycaemia (check glucose 4‑hourly)
  - Steroid-induced psychosis (rare, but monitor)
  - Infection (e.g. oral thrush, reactivation of herpes simplex)
  - Gastric irritation (consider PPI if history of ulcers)
  - RAPD may persist even after vision improvement
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent residual visual deficit (reduced acuity, colour vision, or field loss)
  - Optic atrophy (pale disc) in 30% (visible 4–6 weeks after onset)
  - High risk of developing multiple sclerosis: 30–50% at 5 years if MRI shows white matter lesions; 15% if MRI normal
  - Recurrent optic neuritis (same or other eye) — consider anti-MOG disease or neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Visual recovery:** 80–90% recover to ≥6/9 within 6 months; most improvement in first 2–3 weeks. Full recovery less common than partial.
- **Mortality:** Excellent; optic neuritis alone does not reduce life expectancy unless associated with MS (overall MS survival slightly reduced).
- **MS risk:** Highest in young women with typical symptoms and MRI lesions; all patients should be counselled about MS risk and monitored.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always test colour vision and swinging light** — they are more sensitive than visual acuity for diagnosing and monitoring.
- **If you suspect GCA, give steroids before biopsy** — do not delay; biopsy can be performed up to 2 weeks after starting steroids.
- **Document the RAPD** — it is key evidence of optic nerve damage; also document Ishihara score for both eyes.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe oral steroids without IV methylprednisolone** in severe acute cases — risk of increased MS relapse rate.
- **Do not discharge without MRI brain/orbits** — missing MS diagnosis delays DMT and increases risk of future disability.
- **Do not start steroids if diagnosis unclear** — steroids can worsen infections (e.g. fungal sinusitis causing compressive optic neuropathy) and mask tumour progression. Imaging is essential.
- **Do not neglect VTE prophylaxis** — admitted patients with reduced mobility (even if only for steroid infusion) need LMWH.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Vision worsens after initial improvement; new eye pain or double vision; new neurological symptoms (weakness, numbness, vertigo) — may herald MS relapse.
- **Return if:** Signs of steroid side effects: severe headache, sudden weight gain, shortness of breath (may be fluid overload or pulmonary embolism).
- **Follow-up:** Neuro-ophthalmology clinic in 2–4 weeks; neurology referral for MS risk assessment (MRI, LP). Advise to avoid driving during acute episode — must meet DVLA requirements (visual acuity and field standards).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ophthalmology / neurology if:**
  - Bilateral vision loss or no improvement after 3 days of steroids
  - Development of other cranial nerve signs or papilloedema (consider compressive mass)
  - Suspected GCA — need urgent rheum/ophthalmology for biopsy plan
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any vital sign derangement suggesting sepsis or intracranial catastrophe
  - Deteriorating consciousness (GCS <14) — consider meningitis or pituitary apoplexy
  - Anaphylaxis to IV steroids (rare but possible)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - GCS ≤13 → immediate CT head and neurosurgical consult
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → increase observation frequency and call medical registrar

rawMarkdown:
# Optic Neuritis
**One-liner:** Acute demyelinating inflammation of the optic nerve, typically in young adults (20–50y), strongly associated with multiple sclerosis; threatens vision but rarely fatal.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA) in patients >50y** — can rapidly cause bilateral irreversible blindness *and* aortic dissection/stroke.  
  *Single finding:* “jaw claudication”, new headache, scalp tenderness, or ESR >50 (but never delay steroids while waiting).  
- **Compressive optic neuropathy** (pituitary apoplexy, intracranial mass, aneurysm) — can cause rapid vision loss and fatal brainstem compression.  
  *Single finding:* “bitemporal hemianopia” or “other cranial nerve palsies” on examination → immediate imaging.
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** (infectious or granulomatous) — if fever, meningism, or altered consciousness, treat empirically before scanning.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute or subacute monocular vision loss (hours to days)
  - Pain on eye movement (retrobulbar pain, worse with adduction)
  - Reduced colour vision (desaturation of red) — often earliest symptom
  - “Blurring” or “fogging” in one eye; occasionally visual flashes (photopsia) with movement
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) — present in unilateral cases, even without visible optic disc swelling
  - Reduced visual acuity (Snellen, pinhole)
  - Reduced colour vision (Ishihara plates)
  - Visual field defect (central scotoma most common)
  - Fundoscopy: normal in 2/3 of cases (retrobulbar neuritis); optic disc swelling in anterior (papillitis) cases
  - No proptosis, no conjunctival injection, no ophthalmoplegia (unless MS brainstem lesion)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Optic neuritis (demyelinating):** young adult, pain on movement, RAPD, normal fundus or disc swelling, no fever — classical.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** age >50, headache, jaw claudication, ESR/CRP elevated, fundus may show pale disc/arteritic AION.
- **Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION):** older (>50), often hypertensive/diabetic, no pain, altitudinal field defect, no RAPD? (often subtle), disc oedema with haemorrhages.
- **Compressive lesion (e.g. pituitary, aneurysm, meningioma):** slow onset or apoplexy; other neuro signs (CN III, IV, VI); visual field bitemporal or homonymous; imaging essential.
- **Infectious/inflammatory (sarcoid, syphilis, lupus):** bilateral or recurrent, uveitis, systemic symptoms; serology + LP.
- **Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON):** male predominance, acute painless vision loss, no recovery, family history (maternal), mitochondrial mutation.

**Splitter:** *The single feature that bifurcates GCA from demyelinating optic neuritis:* **Age >50 + headache/jaw claudication** → treat as GCA (temporal artery biopsy, start steroids immediately). If under 40 and no systemic symptoms, it’s almost always demyelinating.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart) each eye separately
  - Colour vision (Ishihara plates) — often first to drop
  - RAPD (swinging light test) — key
  - Fundoscopy (direct ophthalmoscopy) — look for disc swelling, haemorrhages, pallor
  - Check pupils, eye movements, confrontational visual fields
  - If age >50 or suspect GCA: ESR + CRP (stat)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - Autoantibodies (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA) if suspect lupus/vasculitis
  - ACE (if sarcoid possible)
  - Syphilis serology (TPPA/VDRL)
  - Anti-MOG antibodies (if recurrent, bilateral, or treatment-resistant) — send to specialist lab
  - Consider Lyme serology (if tick exposure)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain + orbits with gadolinium (thin cuts through optic nerves) — look for optic nerve enhancement (demyelination) and white matter lesions suggestive of MS
  - **Second-line:** CT head/orbits if MRI contraindicated or to rule out compressive lesion urgently
  - **Consider:** CT venogram if suspect cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (rare)
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture: if atypical features (fever, bilateral, rapid progression, immunosuppressed) — check opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands (matching CSF-serum bands suggest MS)
  - Visual evoked potentials (VEP): delayed P100 latency — often delayed even after clinical recovery; useful if diagnosis uncertain
  - Optical coherence tomography (OCT): shows retinal nerve fibre layer thinning later; not acute
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in all women of childbearing age (rules out CN II palsy from eclampsia, and affects treatment choice) **AND** **ESR/CRP in all patients >50** to not miss GCA.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If GCA suspected (age >50, headache, jaw claudication, ESR/CRP elevated) — **start steroids immediately** (prednisolone 40–60mg PO or IV methylprednisolone 500mg–1g) **before** biopsy — do not wait for results.
  - If compressive lesion suspected (other cranial nerve signs, visual field bitemporal) — urgent MRI/CT and neurosurgical referral.
  - If infection suspected (fever, meningism) — start empirical antibiotics (ceftriaxone + aciclovir) and perform LP.
  - Otherwise, for isolated demyelinating optic neuritis: no emergency intervention needed; confirm diagnosis and start steroids within 1 week if vision severely affected.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Only if vision significantly impaired** (e.g. acuity <6/12, dense field defect) or bilateral → **consider high-dose IV methylprednisolone** (1g IV daily for 3 days) followed by oral prednisolone taper (starting 50mg/day, reduce 10mg every 3 days over 2–4 weeks) — *use [check local policy] for specific steroid regimen.*
  - If no severe vision loss, no benefit from steroids — observe.
  - Monitor visual acuity daily, pain improvement.
  - Arrange MRI brain/orbits if not already done.
  - Referral to neurology / neuro-ophthalmology if available.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Once acute episode resolves, **refer to neurology** for MS risk assessment (MRI brain for dissemination in space/time, LP for oligoclonal bands)
  - If MRI shows MS‑typical lesions → consider disease‑modifying therapy (DMT) after neurology review; vitamin D supplementation often advised (evidence incomplete but safe)
  - If recurrent optic neuritis without MS → anti‑MOG antibody testing; may need immunosuppression.
  - Visual rehabilitation if residual deficit.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for steroids):**
  - Normal saline if IV methylprednisolone given; ensure good oral intake (steroids cause fluid retention and hyperglycaemia)
  - Capillary blood glucose monitoring (4‑hourly while on high-dose steroids) — check for steroid-induced diabetes
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (all admitted patients with reduced mobility)
  - Diet: no specific restriction; avoid high sugar
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, consider 4‑hourly during steroid infusion

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (severe optic neuritis):**
  - IV methylprednisolone 1g in 250mL 0.9% sodium chloride over 1–2 hours daily for 3 days. Followed by:
  - Oral prednisolone 50mg once daily for 7–10 days, then taper (e.g. reduce by 10mg every 3–4 days).
  - *Check local policy for exact oral taper regimen; some units use no taper after short IV course.*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Oral prednisolone monotherapy (1mg/kg/day for 7–14 days) if IV not appropriate — less studied but used.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Systemic steroids contraindicated in active infection (treat infection first), uncontrolled diabetes (monitor glucose closely), severe psychiatric illness (may worsen), uncontrolled hypertension.
  - Caution with NSAIDs (increased GI bleed risk with steroids).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment for methylprednisolone or prednisolone in renal impairment. Hepatic impairment may require dose reduction — discuss with pharmacy.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **“Giving oral prednisolone alone (without IV methylprednisolone) in severe acute optic neuritis with vision <6/60”** — oral steroids in high doses have been shown to increase relapse risk in MS (the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial); always use IV methylprednisolone for severe cases. Also, **failing to monitor blood glucose** in a non-diabetic patient on high-dose steroids can lead to hyperglycaemic emergencies.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Steroid-induced hyperglycaemia (check glucose 4‑hourly)
  - Steroid-induced psychosis (rare, but monitor)
  - Infection (e.g. oral thrush, reactivation of herpes simplex)
  - Gastric irritation (consider PPI if history of ulcers)
  - RAPD may persist even after vision improvement
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent residual visual deficit (reduced acuity, colour vision, or field loss)
  - Optic atrophy (pale disc) in 30% (visible 4–6 weeks after onset)
  - High risk of developing multiple sclerosis: 30–50% at 5 years if MRI shows white matter lesions; 15% if MRI normal
  - Recurrent optic neuritis (same or other eye) — consider anti-MOG disease or neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD)

### Prognosis
- **Visual recovery:** 80–90% recover to ≥6/9 within 6 months; most improvement in first 2–3 weeks. Full recovery less common than partial.
- **Mortality:** Excellent; optic neuritis alone does not reduce life expectancy unless associated with MS (overall MS survival slightly reduced).
- **MS risk:** Highest in young women with typical symptoms and MRI lesions; all patients should be counselled about MS risk and monitored.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always test colour vision and swinging light** — they are more sensitive than visual acuity for diagnosing and monitoring.
- **If you suspect GCA, give steroids before biopsy** — do not delay; biopsy can be performed up to 2 weeks after starting steroids.
- **Document the RAPD** — it is key evidence of optic nerve damage; also document Ishihara score for both eyes.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe oral steroids without IV methylprednisolone** in severe acute cases — risk of increased MS relapse rate.
- **Do not discharge without MRI brain/orbits** — missing MS diagnosis delays DMT and increases risk of future disability.
- **Do not start steroids if diagnosis unclear** — steroids can worsen infections (e.g. fungal sinusitis causing compressive optic neuropathy) and mask tumour progression. Imaging is essential.
- **Do not neglect VTE prophylaxis** — admitted patients with reduced mobility (even if only for steroid infusion) need LMWH.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Vision worsens after initial improvement; new eye pain or double vision; new neurological symptoms (weakness, numbness, vertigo) — may herald MS relapse.
- **Return if:** Signs of steroid side effects: severe headache, sudden weight gain, shortness of breath (may be fluid overload or pulmonary embolism).
- **Follow-up:** Neuro-ophthalmology clinic in 2–4 weeks; neurology referral for MS risk assessment (MRI, LP). Advise to avoid driving during acute episode — must meet DVLA requirements (visual acuity and field standards).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ophthalmology / neurology if:**
  - Bilateral vision loss or no improvement after 3 days of steroids
  - Development of other cranial nerve signs or papilloedema (consider compressive mass)
  - Suspected GCA — need urgent rheum/ophthalmology for biopsy plan
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any vital sign derangement suggesting sepsis or intracranial catastrophe
  - Deteriorating consciousness (GCS <14) — consider meningitis or pituitary apoplexy
  - Anaphylaxis to IV steroids (rare but possible)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - GCS ≤13 → immediate CT head and neurosurgical consult
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → increase observation frequency and call medical registrar
